EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A swell from the northwest caused surf of 10 to 16 feet along the north-facing shores of Niihau, Kauai, Oahu, Molokai, and Maui; and 8 to 12 feet along the west-facing shores of Niihau, Kauai, Oahu, and Molokai.  No significant property damage or injuries were reported.

A swell from a strong low far northwest of the islands generated surf of 10 to 20 feet along the north-facing shores of Niihau, Kauai, Oahu, Molokai, and Maui; and 8 to 15 feet along the west-facing shores of Niihau, Kauai, Oahu, and Molokai. There were no reports of serious injuries or property damage.

A swell from a powerful low far northwest of the Aloha State generated surf of 25 to 40 feet along the north- and west-facing shores of Niihau and Kauai, and the north-facing shores of Oahu, Molokai, and Maui; 15 to 25 feet along the west-facing shores of Oahu and Molokai; 10 to 20 feet along the west-facing shores of the Big Island of Hawaii; 10 to 15 feet along the west-facing shores of Maui; and 6 to 12 feet along the west-facing shores of Lanai and Kahoolawe, and the south-facing shores of Oahu.||Officials closed several beaches on the individual isles ahead of the high surf.  After the surf arrived, reports came in that indicated damages on the Big Island to: a wharf, massage huts near a beach, sea walls, a lifeguard tower, and water lines to restrooms; and on Maui, damages to the Kaloko Pond at Kaloko-Honokohau National Historic Park.  Also, the surging high water brought debris ashore in many locales that affected shoreline roads and parking areas.  The cost of the all the damages had not been calculated.  No serious injuries were reported.

A swell from a powerful low far northwest of the Aloha State generated surf of 25 to 40 feet along the north- and west-facing shores of Niihau and Kauai, and the north-facing shores of Oahu, Molokai, and Maui; 15 to 25 feet along the west-facing shores of Oahu and Molokai; 10 to 20 feet along the west-facing shores of the Big Island of Hawaii; 10 to 15 feet along the west-facing shores of Maui; and 6 to 12 feet along the west-facing shores of Lanai and Kahoolawe, and the south-facing shores of Oahu.||Officials closed several beaches on the individual isles ahead of the high surf.  After the surf arrived, reports came in that indicated damages on the Big Island to: a wharf, massage huts near a beach, sea walls, a lifeguard tower, and water lines to restrooms; and on Maui, damages to the Kaloko Pond at Kaloko-Honokohau National Historic Park.  Also, the surging high water brought debris ashore in many locales that affected shoreline roads and parking areas.  The cost of the all the damages had not been calculated.  No serious injuries were reported.

A swell from a powerful low in the southern hemisphere generated surf of 6 to 12 feet along the south-facing shores of all the islands. Lifeguards on Oahu alone rescued 90 people over the 1st and 2nd of the month, and performed more than 1350 preventive actions. On the Big Island of Hawaii, a man died after apparently being overcome by high surf on the 1st. There were no reports of serious property damage.

A blaze charred 400 acres of dry brush along Pokii Ridge, upslope from Kokee Road, on Kauai.  It did not threaten any homes, cause any serious damage, or result in any significant injuries.  The cause of the fire was unknown.

With a weak trade flow and daytime heating, heavy rain drenched leeward areas of Oahu and portions of West Maui.  The showers caused small stream and drainage ditch flooding, and ponding on roadways.  No significant property damage or injuries were reported.

A lightning strike near Keaau on the Big Island of Hawaii took the roof off a home.

With a moist air mass over the islands, warm ocean temperatures, and low level instability; heavy showers and thunderstorms blossomed and brought varying levels of flooding to parts of the Aloha State.

Upper level instability acted on low level moisture to produce heavy showers and isolated thunderstorms over the western part of the Aloha State.  The rain was heavy enough to cause flash flooding on Kauai near Hanalei as the Hanalei River overflowed onto Kuhio Highway near the Hanalei Bridge.  However, there were no reports of serious injuries or property damage.

Two fires in central Oahu near Wahiawa scorched about 500 acres of brush.  The blazes forced the evacuation of some buildings on a Navy installation and around 100 homes for a time.  Schools in the area were closed for a couple of days because of smoke.  However, no significant property damage was reported.  One Honolulu Fire Department firefighter sustained a leg injury and was hospitalized, though the problem was not life-threatening.  The cause of the fires was not known.

A fire blackened nearly 5000 acres of brush near Kawaihae in the leeward section of the Big island of Hawaii.  The blaze threatened two neighborhoods, and several homes were evacuated for a day.  Traffic also had to be rerouted as the fire jumped the North Kohala Road, and smoke plumes closed other roads in the area.  However, no serious injuries were reported.  A pile of lumber awaiting shipment near Kawaihae Harbor ignited as the fire moved through, though the cost of damages was not available.  The cause of the fire was unknown.

A swell from Tropical Storm Hilda caused surf of 8 to 16 feet along the east-facing shores of Maui and the Big Island of Hawaii.  The high surf kept lifeguards busy with warnings to beach-goers and assists to those struggling in the water.  However, no significant injuries or property damage were reported.

A fire charred almost 200 acres of brush in North Kona in a leeward section of the Big Island of Hawaii.  Some residents of Wainani Estates and Kona Acres, very close to the burn area, helped battle the blaze near their homes with garden hoses. A swell from Tropical Storm Guillermo generated surf of 10 to 20 feet along the east-facing shores of Kauai, Oahu, Molokai, Maui, and the Big Island of Hawaii.  The exceptionally high surf forced county officials to close beaches in Maui and on the Big Island on the 5th.  The high water also brought debris onto coastal roads near the inundation areas.  There were no reports of serious property damage or injuries.

Trade wind showers in windward East Maui caused small stream and drainage ditch flooding, and ponding on roadways.  There were no reports of serious property damage or injuries.

Low-level instability in the area helped generate heavy trade showers on Oahu, and showers and thunderstorms on the Big Island of Hawaii.  At the highest elevation on the Big Island, near and at the summit of Mauna Kea, one to two inches of snow fell.  The precipitation at the lower levels produced ponding on  roadways, and small stream and drainage ditch flooding.  The snow on the Big Island forced the closure of the Summit Road for a time.  There were no reports of significant injuries or property damage.

A fire blackened about 200 acres of brush south of Waimea on the Big Island of Hawaii, on Parker Ranch Land.  The blaze did not threaten any structures or endangered plant life.  Its cause was unknown.  No serious property damage or injuries were reported.

A fire burned 40 acres of brush behind the Lihue Airport on Kauai.  The smoke from the blaze forced airfield controllers to divert three flights from Lihue to Honolulu, with one of the flights arriving from the mainland.  However, there were no reports of any significant injuries or property damage.  The cause of the fire was undetermined.

With gentle winds and afternoon heating, heavy showers developed over the leeward mountain slopes on the Big Island of Hawaii, mainly in North and South Kohala.  The rain caused small stream and drainage ditch flooding, and ponding on roadways.  No serious property damage or injuries were reported.
Part of windward Kauai was added to the severe category, D2, of drought.  Low precipitation has been a problem in the designated area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High running water at Wailuku River's Boiling Pots on the Big Island resulted in one drowning fatality after the swimmer was pulled downstream and didn't make it out.  The other swimmer was able to make it to the river bank downstream.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fire/rescue crews responded to a 5:50 p.m. alarm Monday (Sept 14) to Wailuku River Boiling Pots State Park for a missing swimmer.|A witness said he was swimming in the upper pond at Boiling Pots with a 62-year-old woman when both began to be pulled downstream by the fast-moving current. Both attempted to swim towards the river bank but the woman didn���t make it out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A swell from a deep and powerful low far northwest of the islands generated surf of 20 to 35 feet along the north- and west-facing shores of Niihau and Kauai, and the north-facing shores of Oahu, Molokai, and Maui; and 10 to 18 feet along the west-facing shores of Oahu and the north-facing shores of the Big Island of Hawaii.  A catamaran entering Haleiwa Boat Harbor on the North Shore of Oahu on the 15th was hit by 15- to 18-foot waves, with several people being thrown overboard.  One woman was injured seriously enough to be transported in stable condition to a hospital.  No other injuries were reported.  There were no reports of significant property damage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Along with falling snow and generally icy conditions, the high winds over the summits on the Big Island resulted in the Summit Road being closed from the Visitors Center to the observatories from Jan. 2nd until Jan. 10th. A cold front with gusty winds, the second in a week, moved through the Aloha State.  This system also brought more precipitation than the previous one.  Two injuries during this episode were severe enough to send two individuals to the hospital.  As was the case during the earlier frontal passage, the winds brought down power lines, power poles, and trees; damaged roofs, and closed roadways because of debris on the surfaces.  The total cost of damages was not available.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Firefighter injured when attempting to secure roof materials in Kaneohe in windward Oahu.  A strong gust lifted a tarp that the individual was on, which subsequently came back down on the roof in a hard manner.  The man was taken to a hospital in serious condition.

A swell from a deep and powerful low far northwest of the islands generated surf of 20 to 35 feet along the north- and west-facing shores of Niihau and Kauai, and the north-facing shores of Oahu, Molokai, and Maui; and 10 to 18 feet along the west-facing shores of Oahu and the north-facing shores of the Big Island of Hawaii.  A catamaran entering Haleiwa Boat Harbor on the North Shore of Oahu on the 15th was hit by 15- to 18-foot waves, with several people being thrown overboard.  One woman was injured seriously enough to be transported in stable condition to a hospital.  No other injuries were reported.  There were no reports of significant property damage.

A lava flow near Pahoa in the Puna District on the Big Island triggered several dry-brush fires.  The total area affected was about 800 acres.  The blazes did not damage any structures or rare vegetation.  Firefighters were able to contain the biggest fires and douse the smaller ones.  No serious property damage or injuries were reported.

A fire that blackened around 500 acres of native vegetation in the Oahu Forest National Wildlife Refuge continued to burn through the 30th.  The blaze, above Mililani Mauka in central Oahu, was difficult to extinguish because of its remote location.  Firefighters were battling the fire by dropping water from helicopters.  The cost of damages to the native plants and trees was not available.  No significant injuries were reported.  The cause of the blaze was unknown.

An upper trough that persisted over the islands caused heavy rain over parts of Maui and Molokai, and a winter storm at the summits of Mauna Kea and Mauna Loa on the Big Island of Hawaii.  The rain produced ponding on roadways, and small stream and drainage ditch flooding.  The volcano summits received from 1/2 to 2 feet of snow along the Summit Road.  However, no serious property damage or injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Summit Road received from 1/2 to 2 feet of snow over the 12-hour period.

A fire blackened about 200 acres of dry brush near mile marker 56 on Highway 11 in the Kau District on the Big Island of Hawaii.  The blaze closed the highway in the area for most of Monday (May 4th) afternoon.  No damages or injuries were reported.  The cause of the blaze was not known.

A digging upper trough, a nearby jet stream, and low-level instability generated blustery weather over the summits of Mauna Kea and Mauna Loa on the Big Island of Hawaii.  The Summit Road was closed due to high winds and blowing snow that created white-out conditions.  There were no reports of significant property damage or injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: White-out conditions with high winds around 50 knots and blowing snow.  The Summit Road was closed.

A fire charred 15 acres of dry brush in Naalehu in the Kau District on the Big Island of Hawaii.  It destroyed one structure, an unpermitted home, in the Green Sands Subdivision.  The blaze also forced the evacuation of 10 other homes in the immediate vicinity, near Pele and Mark Twain roads.  The fire was caused by someone who had been burning rubbish in a 55-gallon drum that subsequently got away from him.  There were no reports of serious injuries.  The cost of the damages had not been determined.||The fire had been sustained for a good length of time by moderate to fresh winds in the area, as high as 20 to 25 mph at times.

A cold front with gusty winds, the second in a week, moved through the Aloha State.  This system also brought more precipitation than the previous one.  Two injuries during this episode were severe enough to send two individuals to the hospital.  As was the case during the earlier frontal passage, the winds brought down power lines, power poles, and trees; damaged roofs, and closed roadways because of debris on the surfaces.  The total cost of damages was not available.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines and trees were reported downed in Hawi town in the North Kohala District on the Big Island of Hawaii.

The hikers were shuttled out of trouble by the Kauai Fire Department's Air One helicopter.  There were no reports of serious injuries or property damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A swell from a powerful low in the southern hemisphere generated surf of 6 to 12 feet along the south-facing shores of all the islands. Lifeguards on Oahu alone rescued 90 people over the 1st and 2nd of the month, and performed more than 1350 preventive actions. On the Big Island of Hawaii, a man died after apparently being overcome by high surf on the 1st. There were no reports of serious property damage.

A blaze burned around 80 acres of dry brush in the Makakilo area in leeward Oahu.  The fire threatened homes for a time, and 50 residents evacuated to a shelter at the Makakilo Community Park.  In the end, the fire did not damage any structures or result in any significant injuries.  Its cause was unknown.

A fire blackened about 70 acres of dry brush on the sea-side of Kuhio Highway in Hanamaulu on the island of Kauai.  The blaze did not lead to serious injuries or property damage.  It was caused when a tractor mower became entangled in barbed wire, creating a spark that ignited the fire.

A fire blackened about 250 acres of sugar cane in Pukalani on the Valley Isle of Maui.  The cane was one week away from being harvested.  The fire did not threaten any structures, and no injuries were reported.  Fire officials believe the blaze was intentionally set.

A front moving through the islands generated heavy showers from Oahu to Maui.  The rain caused ponding on roadways, and small stream and drainage ditch flooding.  No significant property damage or injuries were reported.

A swell from the north-northeast generated surf of 10 to 20 feet along the north-facing shores, and 6 to 12 feet along the east-facing shores, of all the islands except Lanai.  On the 3rd, the surf was high enough to close Bayfront Highway in Hilo on the Big Island of Hawaii for a time, and 7 kayakers had to be rescued from waters off Milolii Beach along the Na Pali Coast on Kauai because of the high surf.  However, there were no reports of serious property damage or injuries.

A band of moisture moving from the southeast brought heavy showers to windward areas of the Big Island of Hawaii.  The rain caused ponding on roadways, and small stream and drainage ditch flooding.  No significant injuries or property damage were reported.

A slow-moving surface trough, heading east to west, triggered heavy showers and thunderstorms over Maui and Oahu.  The downpours in the windward area of Oahu on the 12th produced flash flooding.  Otherwise, the rainfall caused small stream and drainage ditch flooding, and ponding on roadways.  There were no reports of significant injuries.  Property damage reports were not available.

A swell from Hurricane Olaf produced surf of 10 to 20 feet along the east-facing shores of the Big Island of Hawaii, 8 to 12 feet along the east-facing shores of Maui, and 6 to 9 feet along the south-facing shores of all the isles.  A stretch of Highway 137, Kalapana-Kapoho Road, in the Puna District on the Big island, was inundated by eight inches of water from the high surf on the 22nd.  However, no significant injuries or property damage were reported.

A band of showers riding on veering winds moved over parts of Oahu at the end of the month.  The rain caused small stream and drainage ditch flooding, and ponding on roadways.  There were no reports of serious injuries or property damage.

After a weak front moved through the Aloha State, a moist flow from the north-northeast became established and produced heavy, terrain-enhanced, showers to parts of Maui County and the Big Island of Hawaii.  The precipitation was heavy enough to generate a flash flood in the northern part of the Big Island on the 6th.  However, no serious injuries or property damage were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Kuakini Highway was closed between Palani and Kaiwi Streets near Kailua-Kona on the leeward side of the Big Island because of a tree blown onto power lines.  Some residents were without power for a time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As moisture from former Tropical Cyclone Nora moved through the island chain, showers and isolated thunderstorms were prevalent from the Big Island to Oahu.  The rain produced flash flooding in the Hamakua District on the Big Island near Laupahoehoe on the 16th.  Elsewhere, the precipitation mainly caused ponding on roadways, and small stream and drainage ditch flooding.  No serious injuries or property damage were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 19 was closed for a time by high water on the roadway north of Laupahoehoe, at Kaawalii Gulch, in the Hamakua District on the Big Island.

A swell from a powerful low far northwest of the Aloha State generated surf of 15 to 25 feet along the north-facing shores of all the islands except Lanai; 10 to 20 feet along the west-facing shores of Niihau, Kauai, Oahu, Molokai, and Maui; and 8 to 12 feet along the west-facing shores of the Big Island.  In the morning hours in the Hilo area on the  Big Island on the 28th, the surf closed Kalanianaole Avenue between Kioea and Kolea Streets as water poured over the roadway.||A large wave near Kaena Point on Oahu swept three men into the water on the 27th, where one died and the others were injured.  On Kauai on the same day, a 33-foot sailing vessel ran aground in the high surf after its motor failed.  The vessel beached on the west side of Hanalei Bay at Waipa.  The boat's owner injured himself trying to leave the boat.  The cost of any damages was not available.

A weakening surface trough over the eastern part of the Aloha State, in combination with an upper trough, helped set off heavy rain in Maui and on the Big Island of Hawaii, and isolated thunderstorms. The showers produced small stream and drainage ditch flooding, and ponding on roadways. No significant property damage or injuries were reported.

Upper level instability acted on low level moisture to produce heavy showers and isolated thunderstorms over the western part of the Aloha State.  The rain was heavy enough to cause flash flooding on Kauai near Hanalei as the Hanalei River overflowed onto Kuhio Highway near the Hanalei Bridge.  However, there were no reports of serious injuries or property damage.

Low-level instability in the area helped generate heavy trade showers on Oahu, and showers and thunderstorms on the Big Island of Hawaii.  At the highest elevation on the Big Island, near and at the summit of Mauna Kea, one to two inches of snow fell.  The precipitation at the lower levels produced ponding on  roadways, and small stream and drainage ditch flooding.  The snow on the Big Island forced the closure of the Summit Road for a time.  There were no reports of significant injuries or property damage.

With low- and high-level features destabilizing a very moist, tropical air mass; intense showers and isolated thunderstorms developed over parts of the Aloha State. The rain caused small stream and drainage ditch flooding, ponding on roadways, and areas of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water over the road forced the closure of Piilani Highway at mile marker 29 in the Nuu area, not far from Kaupo on the island of Maui.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective system developed near the Aloha State and moved through the area as deep, tropical moisture became entrenched over the region as Tropical Depression Kilo passed south of the islands.  Heavy rain, thunderstorms, and flash flooding were the result of all these ingredients.

A swell from Hurricane Ignacio and one out of the southern hemisphere generated surf of 10 to 20 feet along the east-facing shores of all the islands except Lanai, and 6 to 8 feet along the south-facing shores of all islands, respectively.  The surf meant extra work for lifeguards, with warnings and assistance to those in distress.  And the unusually high surf on east shores occasionally deposited sand and other debris on roadways along the coast.|| There were no reports of serious property damage.  As for injuries, a man about 50 years old was found unconscious in the water near the canoe hale at Ala Moana Beach Park on Oahu on the 7th.  He was treated by paramedics at then transported to an area hospital in critical condition.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: One foot of water flooded the intersection of Liliha, Dillingham, and North King Street in Honolulu on the island of Oahu. More flash flooding was reported at the intersection of Dillingham and Alakawa, and Liliha Street was closed in both directions from King to Vineyard Boulevard because of excessive ponding on the roadway. In the Iwilei section of Honolulu, Dole Cannery and surrounding offices had to be evacuated due to flooding on the first floor, including rooms with electrical equipment.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist flow from the east and a system aloft combined to bring heavy showers to leeward sections of the Big Island of Hawaii.

With a front stalling far northwest of the islands, low level winds veered to the southeast over the island chain.  This flow, combined with weak instability, brought heavy rain to eastern parts of Molokai.  The showers caused ponding on roadways, and small stream and drainage ditch flooding.  No significant injuries or property damage were reported.

A swell from a powerful low far northwest of the islands generated surf of 20 to 35 feet along the north-facing, and 10 to 20 feet along the west-facing, shores of Niihau, Kauai, Oahu, Molokai.  Surf reached 20 to 35 feet along the north-facing shores of Maui as well.  Lifeguards and other ocean safety officials provided assistance to surfers and other beachgoers as warranted in the rough conditions.  One 22-year-old surfer nearly drowned (and was taken to a hospital in critical condition) at the Banzai Pipeline on Oahu's North Shore on the 6th because of the dangerous surf.  No other significant injuries, or property damage, were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hualalai Road uphill and makai of Queen Kaahumanu Highway near Kailua-Kona on the Big Island of Hawaii was closed due to flooding. Gusty winds wreaked havoc in parts of Oahu as strong high pressure built far northeast of the the islands.  Trees were downed and roofs were blown off some homes, including in Kailua and Kaimuki. Other neighborhoods that suffered damages were Ewa Beach, Palolo, Wahiawa, Mililani, Kapolei, Pearl City, Manoa, and downtown Honolulu.  Most of the high wind problems occurred on the 19th and 20th of the month.  There were no reports of serious injuries, however.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roof blown off home in Kailua.

And the unusually high surf on east shores occasionally deposited sand and other debris on roadways along the coast.|| There were no reports of serious property damage.  As for injuries, a man about 50 years old was found unconscious in the water near the canoe hale at Ala Moana Beach Park on Oahu on the 7th.  He was treated by paramedics at then transported to an area hospital in critical condition.

Another round of heavy rain and flooding developed over parts of the Aloha State as the remnants of former Hurricane Jimena passed north of the islands.  Warm ocean temperatures and the added instability from the tropical disturbance helped generate deep convection over the area.

A moist flow from the east and a system aloft combined to bring heavy showers to leeward sections of the Big Island of Hawaii.
A moist flow from the east and a system aloft combined to bring heavy showers to leeward sections of the Big Island of Hawaii.

A trade wind swell, along with an east swell generated by Tropical Storm Niala, produced surf of 6 to 10 feet along the east-facing shores of all the main isles except Lanai.  There were no reports of serious property damage or injuries.

Severe drought, D2 category in the Drought Monitor, held on through the first week of September in parts of Kauai and Maui.  However, the great amount of rainfall over most of the state finally lessened the severity from D2 to D1, moderate drought, and then to the D0 category, abnormally dry, by the end of the month.
